items.question: Define mutual information I(X; Y) in terms of entropy.
items.answer: I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X | Y) = H(Y) - H(Y | X) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X, Y). It measures the average reduction in uncertainty about X when Y is known, in the same units as H (bits if log base 2).

items.question: Two fair independent coins X and Y. What is I(X; Y)?
items.answer: 0 bits. Independence gives H(X, Y) = H(X) + H(Y) = 2 bits, so I(X; Y) = 1 + 1 - 2 = 0.

items.question: Y is a perfect copy of fair coin X. What is I(X; Y)?
items.answer: 1 bit. H(X) = H(Y) = 1 bit, joint outcomes are (0,0) and (1,1) only so H(X, Y) = 1 bit, hence I(X; Y) = 1 + 1 - 1 = 1 bit.

items.question: How does mutual information relate to KL divergence?
items.answer: I(X; Y) = D_KL(P_{X,Y} || P_X P_Y): KL divergence between the joint distribution and the product of marginals. It is zero exactly when X and Y are independent.

items.question: Why can I(X; Y) be large while Pearson correlation is near zero?
items.answer: Correlation measures linear association. Non-linear deterministic or probabilistic relationships (e.g., Y = X^2) can yield I(X; Y) > 0 while linear correlation is ~0 because dependence is not captured by a single linear slope.
